district: Mina / Minet el-Hosn, Beirut Central District

Gene_A.description: Gene_A records the site conditions, urban context, environmental conditions, functional needs, and contextual reasoning of the precedent.
Gene_A.location: Beirut Terraces is located in Beirut Central District, within the Mina / Minet el-Hosn area near the Mediterranean shoreline.
Gene_A.urban_context: The project belongs to a dense coastal redevelopment district where luxury residential towers, hotels, commercial buildings, marina-related development, and historical urban fragments coexist.
Gene_A.site_conditions: The site is located in a high-value urban coastal context close to the Mediterranean Sea, Zaitunay Bay, Saint George Bay, luxury high-rise developments, commercial programs, and the historically significant Holiday Inn Beirut ruin.

adjacent_uses.east: Beirut Central District blocks, reconstructed mixed-use urban fabric, commercial buildings, hotels, and local streets.
adjacent_uses.west: Ruined Holiday Inn Beirut area, Omar Daouk Street direction, and historical urban fragments of Minet el-Hosn.
adjacent_uses.south: Fouad Najjar Street, downtown residential and commercial blocks, and urban streets leading deeper into Beirut Central District.
adjacent_uses.north: Platinum Tower direction, Zaitunay Bay / Saint George Bay waterfront area, marina-related development, and Mediterranean Sea views.

surrounding_context.east: Reconstructed downtown fabric, commercial and mixed-use buildings, urban streets, hotels, and financial / administrative functions.
surrounding_context.west: Holiday Inn Beirut ruins and historically significant urban remnants related to Beirut's post-war memory.
surrounding_context.south: Beirut Central District blocks, local urban streets, residential and commercial uses, and access toward the inland city fabric.
surrounding_context.north: Waterfront high-rises, marina-related development, Zaitunay Bay / Saint George Bay direction, and open sea views.

Gene_A.climate_conditions: Beirut has a Mediterranean climate with strong sunlight, warm summers, and a strong culture of outdoor living. The project responds with deep terraces, projecting slabs, overhangs, vegetation, and shaded outdoor spaces.
Gene_A.view_conditions: The project has strong view potential toward the Mediterranean Sea, Zaitunay Bay, Saint George Bay, Beirut Central District, surrounding towers, and the city skyline. Terraces frame these views while also creating privacy buffers.
Gene_A.functional_needs: The project provides high-end residential apartments with generous outdoor terraces, flexible layouts, private or semi-private circulation, shaded outdoor living spaces, and sea or city views.
Gene_A.density_or_height_condition: The building stacks approximately 130 apartments in a high-rise format while using non-identical floor plates and terrace variation to avoid the monotony of repeated residential floors.
Gene_A.target_residents: High-end urban residents seeking outdoor living, privacy, sea views, climate comfort, and luxury apartment living in Beirut Central District.

context_steps.subject: surrounding context
context_steps.object: luxury residential towers and historical urban fragments

context_steps.subject: layered facade
context_steps.object: Mediterranean climate and outdoor living culture

Gene_B.description: Gene_B records the design issue, design concept, and overall design strategy.
Gene_B.issue: High-rise residential towers in dense coastal cities often struggle to provide generous outdoor living, privacy, shading, flexible layouts, and a meaningful relationship to climate and view. Standard glass towers can feel sealed, repetitive, and environmentally exposed.
Gene_B.concept: Transform the high-rise apartment tower into a vertical neighborhood of layered terraces, where every residence has a strong indoor-outdoor relationship, access to shade, privacy, vegetation, and views.
Gene_B.strategy: Use projecting and setback slabs to create deep terraces, overhangs, and varied floor plates. Combine modular floor types, flexible non-structural apartment layouts, vegetation screens, and central core organization to produce non-identical apartments and climate-responsive outdoor living.
design_intentions: Make outdoor living central to high-rise residential life.
Use terrace layering to create shade, privacy, and facade depth.
Respond to Beirut's Mediterranean climate through overhangs, vegetation, and shaded outdoor space.
Frame sea and city views while reducing direct exposure to neighboring towers.
Use modular floor plates to create variation rather than repetition.
Blur the boundary between interior rooms and exterior terraces.
Create a vertical neighborhood through layered slabs and varied domestic outdoor spaces.

Gene_C1.description: Gene_C1 records the architectural vocabulary extracted from site context, drawings, diagrams, photos, and renders.

Gene_C2.description: Gene_C2 records semantic spatial relationships and strategy candidates. Each relation follows the format: Subject → Relation (Relation Type) → Object.

semantic_relations.subject: horizontal slab
semantic_relations.description: Projecting and setback slabs create exterior terrace spaces around the apartments.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Make outdoor space central to high-rise living
strategy_candidates.strategy: Use horizontal slabs as inhabitable platforms that generate large terraces rather than small attached balconies.

reasoning.premise: High-rise apartments often lack generous private outdoor space.
reasoning.analysis: Deep slabs can become extensions of domestic life and support outdoor rooms.
reasoning.conclusion: The floor slab becomes the main device for creating high-rise outdoor living.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Create spatial depth in the facade
strategy_candidates.strategy: Project and set back slab edges to produce terraces, shadows, and layered facade conditions.

reasoning.premise: Flat glass facades provide limited environmental and spatial buffering.
reasoning.analysis: Slab depth creates inhabitable layers between interior and city.
reasoning.conclusion: The facade becomes a thick spatial zone rather than a thin envelope.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Vary the slab projection and setback condition so each apartment gains a different terrace relationship.

reasoning.premise: Repeated residential floors can produce monotony.
reasoning.analysis: Variation in terrace slabs creates different unit identities.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower becomes a stack of distinct residences rather than identical floors.

semantic_relations.subject: terrace
semantic_relations.description: Terraces extend indoor living, dining, and bedroom areas outward, creating a continuous indoor-outdoor residential experience.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Connect living and dining rooms directly to deep terraces so residents can use outdoor space as part of daily domestic life.

reasoning.premise: Beirut's climate and culture support outdoor living for much of the year.
reasoning.analysis: A terrace connected to main rooms becomes a usable extension of the home.
reasoning.conclusion: High-rise living gains a Mediterranean indoor-outdoor lifestyle.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Treat terraces as outdoor rooms rather than secondary balconies.

reasoning.premise: Luxury apartments require spacious and flexible living environments.
reasoning.analysis: Deep terraces provide additional living, dining, and gathering areas.
reasoning.conclusion: The apartment feels larger and more connected to its context.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use floor-to-ceiling glazing and continuous terrace surfaces to merge indoor rooms with outdoor platforms.

reasoning.premise: A strong threshold can separate residents from climate and view.
reasoning.analysis: Transparent edges and spatial continuity weaken this separation.
reasoning.conclusion: The residence becomes a continuous indoor-outdoor environment.

semantic_relations.subject: overhang
semantic_relations.description: Large overhangs protect interiors and terraces from direct sunlight, reducing heat gain and creating sheltered outdoor zones.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use projecting slabs as passive shading devices that block direct sun before it reaches glazed interiors.

reasoning.premise: Beirut's Mediterranean climate includes strong sun and warm summers.
reasoning.analysis: Deep slab overhangs reduce solar exposure on glass and terrace surfaces.
reasoning.conclusion: Interior comfort improves through passive shading.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Create semi-covered terrace zones under overhanging slabs.

reasoning.premise: Outdoor spaces can become uncomfortable if fully exposed to sun.
reasoning.analysis: Overhangs create sheltered areas for sitting, dining, and relaxing.
reasoning.conclusion: The terrace becomes more functional throughout the day.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Let the horizontal overhangs define the building's visual language and environmental performance simultaneously.

reasoning.premise: Climate devices are often hidden or treated separately from facade design.
reasoning.analysis: In Beirut Terraces, shading slabs are also the main architectural expression.
reasoning.conclusion: The building identity is generated by climate response.

semantic_relations.subject: vegetation
semantic_relations.description: Planting on terraces acts as a privacy screen between apartments and the surrounding urban context.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Protect privacy in a dense tower district
strategy_candidates.strategy: Place vegetation along terrace edges to filter direct views between apartments and neighboring towers.

reasoning.premise: The project is located in a dense high-rise residential and mixed-use district.
reasoning.analysis: Terrace planting creates a semi-transparent privacy layer.
reasoning.conclusion: Residents can maintain openness while reducing visual exposure.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use plants to create a more domestic and garden-like atmosphere on the terraces.

reasoning.premise: Concrete and glass terraces can feel hard or exposed.
reasoning.analysis: Vegetation adds softness, shade, and sensory richness.
reasoning.conclusion: The terrace becomes more comfortable and residential.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use vegetation to cool, shade, and filter the terrace edge.

reasoning.premise: Outdoor living spaces need protection from heat and glare.
reasoning.analysis: Plants provide shade and a softer microclimate.
reasoning.conclusion: Vegetation supports both comfort and privacy.

semantic_relations.subject: terrace
semantic_relations.description: The terrace becomes a viewing platform that frames views toward the Mediterranean Sea and Beirut skyline.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Use waterfront location as residential value
strategy_candidates.strategy: Orient terraces toward sea and city views where possible.

reasoning.premise: The project is located near the Mediterranean shoreline.
reasoning.analysis: Terraces can act as outdoor viewing platforms.
reasoning.conclusion: The apartment experience is strongly connected to Beirut's coastal context.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use the terrace as a foreground layer before the view opens to the sea or city.

reasoning.premise: Views in dense high-rise districts can be exposed or direct.
reasoning.analysis: A terrace foreground creates depth between the apartment and the distant view.
reasoning.conclusion: The view becomes more spatially layered and private.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Place outdoor dining and lounge areas on terraces with sea or skyline orientation.

reasoning.premise: Outdoor living is more meaningful when connected to landscape or city views.
reasoning.analysis: Terrace use and view orientation reinforce each other.
reasoning.conclusion: The terrace becomes both a social space and a viewing platform.

semantic_relations.subject: central core
semantic_relations.description: The structural and circulation core provides elevators, stairs, services, and vertical organization for the residential tower.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use a central core to organize elevators, stairs, shafts, and service distribution.

reasoning.premise: High-rise apartments require efficient vertical access.
reasoning.analysis: A central core can serve multiple units while freeing the perimeter for apartments and terraces.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower maintains efficiency while maximizing exterior-facing living spaces.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Keep the core stable while allowing apartment walls and terrace layouts to vary around it.

reasoning.premise: The project uses multiple modular floor types and flexible layouts.
reasoning.analysis: A stable core provides order while the surrounding residential layout changes.
reasoning.conclusion: The building can combine structural consistency with unit variation.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use the core to concentrate vertical services and keep living areas oriented toward the facade and terraces.

reasoning.premise: Residential quality depends on perimeter access to light, air, and view.
reasoning.analysis: Concentrating services in the core leaves the perimeter open for domestic space.
reasoning.conclusion: Apartment layouts gain stronger relationships to terraces and views.

semantic_relations.subject: column grid
semantic_relations.description: A regular structural grid and non-structural interior walls allow apartment layouts to remain flexible.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use a structural system that permits non-structural apartment partitions to change across floors.

reasoning.premise: Luxury residential projects often require varied apartment sizes and layouts.
reasoning.analysis: A regular column grid can support flexible interior planning.
reasoning.conclusion: The building can accommodate different residential configurations.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Reduce dependence on fixed repetitive plans
strategy_candidates.strategy: Separate structural logic from interior partition logic.

reasoning.premise: A fixed structural wall system can limit plan variation.
reasoning.analysis: Columns and non-structural walls allow layouts to adapt.
reasoning.conclusion: The project can create non-identical apartments within a repeated tower system.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use flexible internal layouts that can adapt to different residents, market conditions, or apartment combinations.

reasoning.premise: Residential needs change over time.
reasoning.analysis: Flexible partitions make adaptation easier than fixed structural walls.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower gains long-term spatial resilience.

semantic_relations.subject: modular floor type
semantic_relations.description: Five modular floor types are repeated in different combinations, creating non-identical apartments and varied terrace conditions.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use several floor plate types instead of one identical typical floor.

reasoning.premise: Many high-rise towers repeat the same floor plan throughout their height.
reasoning.analysis: Multiple modular floor types create different apartment and terrace relationships.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower supports variety while retaining systematic organization.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Combine modular floors in different sequences so that units gain distinct terrace sizes, exposures, and layouts.

reasoning.premise: Residents value unique spatial conditions in luxury housing.
reasoning.analysis: Modular variation produces different forms of outdoor living and privacy.
reasoning.conclusion: Each apartment can feel individualized within a high-rise system.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Balance construction logic and spatial diversity
strategy_candidates.strategy: Use a limited number of repeatable floor types to generate a larger range of apartment conditions.

reasoning.premise: Completely custom floors can be inefficient, while identical floors can be monotonous.
reasoning.analysis: A controlled modular system creates variation without losing order.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower achieves both efficiency and diversity.

semantic_relations.subject: indoor space
semantic_relations.description: Floor-to-ceiling glazing and terrace continuity blur the boundary between interior rooms and exterior living space.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use large glazed openings between living rooms and terraces.

reasoning.premise: The project aims to integrate inside and outside.
reasoning.analysis: Transparent edges allow rooms and terraces to read as connected spaces.
reasoning.conclusion: Residents experience the terrace as part of the apartment.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Visually extend interior rooms outward to terraces and views.

reasoning.premise: Apartment interiors can feel enclosed in high-rise living.
reasoning.analysis: Connection to terraces increases perceived spatial depth.
reasoning.conclusion: The apartment feels larger and more open.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Allow terraces to function as extensions of living, dining, and leisure areas.

reasoning.premise: Residents use domestic spaces differently throughout the day and season.
reasoning.analysis: Connected terraces create flexible zones for different activities.
reasoning.conclusion: The apartment supports a more adaptable lifestyle.

semantic_relations.subject: layered facade
semantic_relations.description: The facade alternates between open terraces, shaded areas, planted screens, and enclosed living spaces.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Alternate exposed, shaded, planted, and enclosed layers across the facade.

reasoning.premise: Residents need both openness and protection in a warm climate.
reasoning.analysis: Layering allows different degrees of shelter and exposure.
reasoning.conclusion: The facade becomes adaptable to weather, privacy, and use.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use inhabitable facade depth instead of a flat sealed glass wall.

reasoning.premise: Sealed towers limit outdoor domestic life and climate adaptation.
reasoning.analysis: A layered facade creates usable spaces between interior and exterior.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower becomes more climatically and socially livable.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Let the facade's depth, slabs, terraces, and shadows define the tower image.

reasoning.premise: Facade identity is often produced by surface material alone.
reasoning.analysis: Beirut Terraces generates identity from spatial layering.
reasoning.conclusion: The building image comes from inhabitable section rather than applied decoration.

semantic_relations.subject: quarter lobby
semantic_relations.description: Each quarter of the tower has its own lobby and elevator access, reducing shared circulation and increasing residential privacy.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use smaller lobby zones to reduce the number of apartments sharing the same circulation space.

reasoning.premise: Luxury residential towers often require a high degree of privacy.
reasoning.analysis: Limiting shared circulation reduces encounters and increases exclusivity.
reasoning.conclusion: The circulation system supports privacy and high-end residential identity.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Divide elevator access into smaller sectors or quarters of the tower.

reasoning.premise: Large shared corridors can make high-rise living feel anonymous.
reasoning.analysis: Smaller lobby zones create a more private arrival experience.
reasoning.conclusion: Each apartment gains a more individualized residential threshold.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use quarter-based access to allow different apartment sizes and configurations around the core.

reasoning.premise: The project uses flexible layouts and varied unit types.
reasoning.analysis: Separated lobby zones can support different unit groupings.
reasoning.conclusion: Circulation supports both privacy and layout flexibility.

semantic_relations.subject: light and shadow
semantic_relations.description: The changing depth of slabs produces strong horizontal shadows and gives the building its layered identity.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Use projecting slabs and recessed terraces to generate horizontal shadow lines across the facade.

reasoning.premise: The tower needs a strong visual identity within a dense high-rise context.
reasoning.analysis: Light and shadow emphasize the layered slab system.
reasoning.conclusion: The building becomes recognizable through its horizontal stratification.

strategy_candidates.strategy: Let shading elements visibly shape the appearance of the tower.

reasoning.premise: Environmental devices can contribute to architectural expression.
reasoning.analysis: Overhangs create both shade and shadow identity.
reasoning.conclusion: The facade communicates climate response.

strategy_candidates.motivation: Make the tower feel layered and inhabited
strategy_candidates.strategy: Use shadowed terraces to reveal depth, occupation, and spatial complexity.

reasoning.premise: Flat towers can appear abstract and uninhabited.
reasoning.analysis: Shadowed outdoor spaces reveal domestic depth.
reasoning.conclusion: The tower reads as a vertical neighborhood of inhabited layers.

Gene_D.description: Gene_D records possible resident behavior, media feedback, and design reflection. Some items are inferred from spatial configuration and should be verified with additional sources if needed.
Gene_D.resident_behavior: Residents are likely to use terraces as major extensions of the apartment, supporting outdoor dining, lounging, planting, viewing, and social activities. The terrace becomes a primary domestic space rather than a secondary balcony. This is an interpretation based on the drawings and exterior image.
Gene_D.media_feedback: Beirut Terraces is often discussed as a high-rise residential project that rethinks tower living through layered slabs, generous terraces, indoor-outdoor continuity, and climate-responsive design.
Gene_D.designer_reflection: The project demonstrates how high-rise residential architecture can be organized around terrace layering rather than flat facade repetition. Its key contribution is the use of the floor slab as a device for shade, privacy, outdoor living, facade identity, and apartment variation.
limitations_or_notes: Exact unit count, floor count, and program distribution should be checked against official sources before final publication.
The comfort of terraces depends on maintenance, climate, planting, orientation, and resident use.
The privacy strategy depends on vegetation, slab depth, and neighboring building relationships.
The modular floor system is transferable, but its spatial generosity may require high-end residential market conditions.

case_summary.one_sentence_summary: Beirut Terraces is a high-rise residential precedent that transforms the tower into a vertical stack of layered terraces, using projecting slabs, vegetation, flexible layouts, and indoor-outdoor continuity to respond to Mediterranean climate and dense coastal urban living.
case_summary.key_design_problem: How can a high-rise residential tower in a dense coastal city provide generous outdoor space, privacy, climate comfort, sea views, and apartment individuality?
case_summary.key_design_solution: Use projecting and setback floor slabs to create deep terraces, shaded outdoor rooms, vegetation screens, flexible apartment layouts, and a layered facade identity.
transferable_strategies_for_highrise_hou: Use floor slabs as inhabitable terrace generators rather than only structural separators.
Create deep terraces that function as outdoor living rooms.
Use overhangs to shade interior and exterior spaces in warm climates.
Use vegetation as a privacy screen and microclimate device.
Vary modular floor plates to create non-identical apartments.
Use a central core and flexible partitions to support changing apartment layouts.
Use light and shadow from slab depth to create architectural identity.
Frame sea or city views through terrace layers rather than exposing apartments directly.

notes: This JSON is based on manually prepared preprocess files for site context, drawings, photos, and renders. It is ready to be included as Case 06 in the precedent database. Exact project data such as unit count, floor count, height, and program distribution should be checked again before final publication.
